description.title: Methodology
description.subtitle: How LexEval evaluates LLM legal reasoning, step by step
description.intro: LexEval is an LLM legal reasoning evaluation platform. It uses a structured, two-layer pipeline aligned with the RRD (Recursive Rubric Decomposition) methodology to measure how well large language models perform on legal reasoning tasks.
description.twoLayers: The pipeline is divided into two layers that run in strict sequence:
description.layerCoreDesc: Response-conditioned rubric proposal, recursive decompose-filter loop, frozen rubric, and baseline evaluation. This layer must complete before the downstream layer begins.
description.layerDownstreamDesc: Embedding, clustering, centroid judging, and model ranking. This layer consumes the frozen rubric produced by the RRD core workflow.

authors.title: Project Authors
authors.note: Authors are listed alphabetically by last name.
authors.name: Name

modelRegistry.title: Implemented Model Registry
modelRegistry.p1: These are the model roles implemented by the application. Internal rubric and judge models are separated from the user-facing comparison pool.
modelRegistry.comparisonNote: The comparison pool is loaded from /api/v1/evaluations/models so this page reflects the implemented backend allowlist.
modelRegistry.error: Could not load the comparison model pool.
modelRegistry.name: Name

stage1.title: Stage 1 -- Case Ingestion
stage1.p1: The user uploads a legal case as a PDF document. The system extracts the full text using pdfplumber and stores it alongside a legal question as the task definition.
stage1.p2: This text and question form the input for the entire downstream pipeline.

stage2.title: Stage 2 -- Setup Response Generation
stage2.p1: Four setup models each generate 25 responses to the legal question, producing 100 candidate responses total. All calls run concurrently under a semaphore.
stage2.p2: Simultaneously, a reference pair is generated: one strong response and one weak response. This pair is used later for misalignment filtering during rubric refinement.
stage2.modelNote: These models are excluded from the comparison pool to prevent any model from influencing the rubric it will later be evaluated against.

stage3.title: Stage 3 -- Clustering to Conditioning Sample
stage3.p1: The 100 setup responses are embedded with sentence-transformers (all-mpnet-base-v2) and clustered using k-means with k fixed at 8.
stage3.p2: The centroid response per cluster -- the response with the highest cosine similarity to the cluster centroid vector -- is selected. These 8 centroid texts form the conditioning sample passed to the rubric proposal step.
stage3.deviation: LexEval deviation from the RRD paper: the paper uses 8 randomly sampled responses as the conditioning set. LexEval instead generates 100 diverse responses and clusters them to 8 representative centroids, ensuring full coverage of the response space.

stage4.title: Stage 4 -- Initial Rubric Proposal
stage4.p1: The control model proposes an initial rubric conditioned on the legal question and the 8 centroid conditioning responses. The proposal uses low temperature (0.2) and requires JSON output matching the criterion schema.
stage4.p2: Rubric criteria should be informative, comprehensive, and non-redundant. The number of criteria emerges from task complexity; no fixed target count is imposed.

stage5.title: Stage 5 -- Recursive Decompose-Filter Loop
stage5.p1: This is the central methodological component. The loop iterates over depth-0 criteria (initial proposals) and applies decomposition and filtering until a stopping condition is met.
stage5.decompositionDesc: A criterion is evaluated against all 8 centroid texts using binary pass/fail scoring. If it passes more than 2 of the 8 centroids, it is too broad and is decomposed into 2-3 narrower subcriteria.
stage5.misalignmentDesc: Each proposed child criterion is evaluated against the strong and weak reference responses. A criterion is discarded only when it passes the weak reference but fails the strong one, indicating an inverted preference direction.
stage5.redundancyDesc: Each proposed child criterion that passes misalignment filtering is checked against already-accepted criteria. If the control model judges it redundant, it is discarded.
stage5.stoppingDesc: The loop terminates when accumulated discarded proposals reach the rejection threshold, when no broad criteria remain (convergence), or at a hard cap of 20 passes.

stage6.title: Stage 6 -- Whitened-Uniform Weight Assignment
stage6.p1: After the refinement loop, final criterion weights are computed using whitened-uniform (WU) weighting from the RRD paper.
stage6.p2: Each centroid is scored against each criterion using binary evaluation, producing a score matrix. The rubric covariance is estimated, and the whitened-uniform weight vector is derived via eigendecomposition. This ensures that correlated rubric dimensions do not dominate the aggregate score.

stage7.title: Stage 7 -- Comparison Response Generation
stage7.p1: The user selects between 2 and 5 models from the comparison pool. Each model generates 40 responses to the legal question. The total number of responses varies with the number of models selected. Response generation runs asynchronously under a concurrency semaphore.
stage7.exclusionDesc: A model is excluded from the comparison pool if it participates in rubric assembly (setup response generation or the proposal/refinement loop) or in final judging (centroid scoring).

stage8.title: Stage 8 -- Embedding and Clustering
stage8.p1: All comparison responses are embedded with sentence-transformers. The optimal cluster count k is selected data-adaptively via silhouette score maximization, sweeping from max(floor(n_models * 1.5), 4).
stage8.p2: K-means is applied at the selected k, and the centroid response per cluster (highest cosine similarity to the centroid vector) is identified for scoring.

stage9.title: Stage 9 -- Centroid Scoring
stage9.p1: The control model scores each centroid response against the frozen rubric criteria. Scores are collected per criterion (0.0-1.0) and the weighted total is computed.
stage9.p2: Results are reported under three weighting modes in parallel:
stage9.heuristic: Heuristic: RRD-assigned WU weights from Stage 6
stage9.whitened: Whitened Uniform: column-wise z-score normalization across clusters, then uniform average
stage9.resultsDesc: The Results page shows the primary ranking and the weighting comparison side by side.
stage9.primary: Heuristic mode is the primary ranking mode used for the winning cluster and top model.
stage9.sensitivity: Uniform and whitened_uniform are reported as sensitivity checks rather than replacement rankings.
stage9.disagreement: If weighting modes disagree, the case should be treated as a rubric-sensitivity signal, not as a pipeline error.

stage10.title: Stage 10 -- Ranking
stage10.p1: The cluster with the highest aggregate rubric score (under heuristic mode) is the winning cluster. Model share within the winning cluster is computed as the fraction of responses from each model.
stage10.p2: The model with the highest share in the winning cluster is ranked first.

controlModel.title: Control Model
controlModel.p1: deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3 serves as the control model for rubric assembly and final judging. This is a LexEval platform choice, not a paper requirement.

variation.title: Variation Selection
variation.description: Optionally generate a variation of the base question before building the rubric.

judgePanel.title: Judge Panel
judgePanel.description: Select 1–3 internal models as judges. Comparison pool models are not eligible.

zak.title: Zak Review Required
zak.description: No judge panel majority was reached. Disputed clusters:

reviewGate.title: Review Required
reviewGate.description: The FrankInstructions pipeline requires human approval before continuing.
reviewGate.rejected: This rubric was rejected during review.
